Therapist: You're feeling uncertain about your ability to maintain this change over the long term. Can you think of a time when you successfully overcame a challenging situation that you didn't think you could handle?

Patient: There was this one time when our power was cut off during the winter, and my wife and I had to figure out how to cook dinner. We managed to find a way to use a candle and a kettle, and we were okay. But that was just for one night. Quitting smoking is a different thing altogether.

Therapist: You're drawing on a past experience where you and your wife were able to overcome a difficult situation together. Can you imagine how it would feel to have your wife's support in staying smoke-free, and how that might impact your chances of quitting?
Patient: It might make it a bit easier. Maybe having her support would help me stay on track, but what if I fail? She's already stressed about our money situation...

Therapist: You're worried about failing and feeling like it would add to your wife's stress. Would it help to imagine how she would feel if you were to succeed in quitting smoking, and how that could bring a sense of relief and support to your relationship?
Patient: I suppose it's possible. But it's not like I've ever seen anyone else quit for good. I don't know if it's even possible for me.
